coredata.dc:title: Enhancement in photoluminescence performance of carbon-based Fe3O4@ZnO–C nanocomposites
coredata.dc:creator: Astuti,
Arief, Syukri
Muldarisnur, Mulda
Zulhadjri,
Usna, Sri R.A.
coredata.dc:description: This research synthesized a Fe3O4@ZnO–C nanocomposite with a core-shell structure using the coprecipitation method. XRD diffractograms indicate the formation of Fe3O4 and ZnO phases, where Fe3O4 has a cubic crystal system while ZnO has a hexagonal crystal system. The presence of carbon (graphite and carbon quantum dots) in Fe3O4@ZnO–C does not change the crystal structure of Fe3O4@ZnO. TEM images show the formation of a core-shell structure in the Fe3O4@ZnO nanocomposite. FTIR measurements show the stretching vibration of the C–O and CC bonds on the Fe3O4@ZnO–C and Fe3O4@ZnO-CQD samples comes from carbon, meaning that the nanocomposites have been coupled with carbon. The optical properties of the nanocomposites were characterized using Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. The addition of carbon and CQD increases photoluminescence significantly. The Fe3O4@ZnO–C photoluminescence intensity is in the orange emission wavelength range, while Fe3O4@ZnO-CQD is in the green emission wavelength range.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fe3O4@ZnO
Photoluminescence
Nanocomposites
Carbon
Copresipitation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The XRD patterns of Fe3O4, Fe3O4@ZnO, Fe3O4@ZnO–C, and Fe3O4@ZnO-CQD nanocomposites.
TEM image of core-shell Fe3O4@ZnO nanocomposite.
TEM image of (a) Fe3O4@ZnO–C nanocomposite, and (b) Fe3O4@ZnO-CQD nanocomposite.
Photoluminescence spectrum of Fe3O4@ZnO, Fe3O4@ZnO–C, and Fe3O4@ZnO-CQD

head.ce:title: Enhancement in photoluminescence performance of carbon-based Fe3O4@ZnO–C nanocomposites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Faculty of Mathematics and Natural Sciences, Andalas University, Padang, 25163, West Sumatera, Indonesia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Mathematics and Natural Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Faculty of Mathematics and Natural Sciences, Andalas University, Padang, 25163, West Sumatera, Indonesia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Mathematics and Natural Sciences, Andalas University, Padang, 25163, West Sumatera, Indonesia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Mathematics and Natural Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Mathematics and Natural Sciences, Andalas University, Padang, 25163, West Sumatera, Indonesia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This research synthesized a Fe3O4@ZnO–C nanocomposite with a core-shell structure using the coprecipitation method. XRD diffractograms indicate the formation of Fe3O4 and ZnO phases, where Fe3O4 has a cubic crystal system while ZnO has a hexagonal crystal system. The presence of carbon (graphite and carbon quantum dots) in Fe3O4@ZnO–C does not change the crystal structure of Fe3O4@ZnO. TEM images show the formation of a core-shell structure in the Fe3O4@ZnO nanocomposite. FTIR measurements show the stretching vibration of the C–O and CC bonds on the Fe3O4@ZnO–C and Fe3O4@ZnO-CQD samples comes from carbon, meaning that the nanocomposites have been coupled with carbon. The optical properties of the nanocomposites were characterized using Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. The addition of carbon and CQD increases photoluminescence significantly. The Fe3O4@ZnO–C photoluminescence intensity is in the orange emission wavelength range, while Fe3O4@ZnO-CQD is in the green emission wavelength range.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:sections.ce:para: Iron Oxide (Fe3O4) nanoparticles is one of the candidates for magnetic materials being developed as biological aplications material. Over the last decade, research on Fe3O4-based magnetic nanoparticles has focused on producing nanoparticles with particle sizes smaller than 15 nm and coating the particles with biocompatible materials [1–3]. Combining magnetic and luminescent materials results in materials with unique properties and applications, such as biomedical applications. The luminescent materials used are from the lanthanide group [4,5] and carbon/graphene [6,7]. Due to their advantages over fluorescent organic materials, quantum dot semiconductors such as ZnO are the most studied luminescence nanomaterials. Because of its unique optical properties, ZnO is widely used in industry. ZnO has a broad bandgap, a high exciton binding energy, and long-term stability [8]. For this reason, strong bonds (such as ZnO-based interfacial covalent bonds) have more potential to be developed, especially for nanoparticles suspended in biological fluids, as demonstrated by core-shell Fe3O4@ZnO nanocomposites [9,10].

The drawback of the Fe3O4@ZnO nanocomposite is the decrease in luminescence intensity. The decrease is caused by the quenching effect caused by Fe ions, dangling bonds, and defects on the ZnO surface. It is, therefore, necessary to modify Fe3O4@ZnO by coupling material for high photoluminescence properties, which can be used to create materials for bioimaging and other imaging applications. Many researchers have modified Fe3O4@ZnO with other materials such as graphen oxide, chitosan, silica and many others to improve the performance of these materials in various applications such as photodegradation of organic pollutan [11], antibacterial [12], drug delivery [13] photocatalytic degradation [14] etc. However, only a few researchers have developed this material as a bioimaging material by increasing its photoluminescence properties. Although this material has potential in the biomedical field, particularly for bioimaging, We investigated multifunctional nanocomposites combining Fe3O4 and ZnO. We then modified the surface of Fe3O4@ZnO with carbon-based materials, namely graphite and carbon quantum dots. The core-shell structure of Fe3O4-based nanocomposite materials is Fe3O4@ZnO. Fe3O4@ZnO nanocomposite was synthesized using the coprecipitation method. The main objective of this study is to understand the effect of surface modification by carbon atoms on Fe3O4@ZnO on the structure and photoluminescent properties. Characterization methods using an XRD (Bruker D8 Advance) to determine the phase and crystal structure and a PL (Horiba MicO Photoluminescence Microspectrometer) to investigate optical properties. TEM (FEI Tecnai G2 20 S-Twin) was used to analyze the morphology and particle size of the samples, and FTIR (Nicolet iS50 FTIR) was used to determine the formed chemical bonds.

The materials used in this study were FeCl3·6H2O (Merck), FeSO4·7H2O (Merck), NH4OH (Merck), 96% alcohol (Bratachem), glucose (Bratachem), Zn(NO3)·6H2O (Merck), and aquadest (distilled water). The Fe3O4@ZnO nanoparticles were prepared by the coprecipitation method with slight modifications [15–17]. Synthesizing the Fe3O4@ZnO nanocomposite core-shell structure began with synthesizing Fe3O4 powder using the coprecipitation method. The Fe3+(FeCl·6H2O) and Fe2+ (FeSO4·7H2O) with a molar ratio of 2:1 were dissolved and stirred in distilled water to obtain a 0.2 M ferrite solution. Ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH) with a concentration of 3.5 M was added to the solution. The stirring process was carried out using a magnetic stirrer and heated at 100 °C for 5 h until a precipitate was formed. Zn(NO₃)₂ was dissolved in 100 ml of distilled water, and then NH4OH was added. Zn(NO₃)₂ solution was mixed into the first solution and heated at 70 °C for 24 h. The precipitate was washed with alcohol three times and then dried in a furnace at 300 °C for 2 h.

The synthesis of Fe3O4@ZnO–C began with the carbon synthesis process. A total of 0.53 g of PEG and 1.8 g of glucose (1:2) were weighed on a digital balance and dissolved in 25 ml of aquadest. The solution was then stirred using a magnetic stirrer for 30 min at 450 rpm. The solution was dried in an oven for 3 h at 300 °C to form carbon and ground until smooth. The following synthesis process combined Fe3O4@ZnO with carbon. A total of 0.05 g of carbon powder was dissolved into 10 ml aquadest. Next, a 0.4 g of Fe3O4@ZnO powder was mixed into the carbon solution and then stirred using a magnetic stirrer for 1 h at a temperature of 80–90 °C and dried at a temperature of 250 °C for 3 h. The resulting powders are Fe3O4@ZnO/C nanocomposite.

The synthesis of Fe3O4/ZnO-CQD began with the hydrothermal synthesis of CQD. A 0.05 g carbon had been synthesized before was dissolved in 10 ml of aquadest, and the dispersion was transferred into a teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave at 180 °C for 12 h to obtain CQD. CQD was combined with Fe3O4@ZnO to obtain a Fe3O4@ZnO/CQD nanocomposite. The composite was made by adding 0.4 g of Fe3O4/ZnO into the CQD solution, stirring for 2 h using a magnetic stirrer, and then sonicated for 40 min. Centrifugation was carried out for 20 min to remove the solvent. The sediment was finally dried in a furnace at 100 °C for 10 h to obtain Fe3O4/ZnO/CQD nanocomposite powder.

XRD characterized the nanomaterials to analyze the crystal nature, phase, and purity (Fig. 1 ). All these data are matched to the standard ICDD of Fe3O4 systems, ICDD 01-071-6339. Fe3O4 lattice parameters are a = b = c = 8.3153 Å, in agreement with the cubic system of Fe3O4 nanoparticles. The crystalline peaks of Fe3O4 observed at the 2θ positions 30.4593°, 35.7909°, 43.3784°, 53.9629°, 57.3681°, 63.2529°, and 90.5352° can be assigned to the indexed planes of Miller (220), (311), 400), (422), (511), (440), and (731). The Fe3O4@ZnO sample was identified as Fe3O4, and the ZnO crystalline structures were simple cubic and hexagonal wurtzite, respectively. ICDD standard data with reference code 01-075-7917 shows the ZnO phase has a hexagonal crystal structure with lattice parameters a = 3.2525 Å, b = 3.2525 Å, and c = 5.2111 Å. The crystalline peaks of ZnO are observed at the 2θ positions of 31.774°, 34.4273°, 36.2561°, 47.5718°, 67.9733°, 69.1152°, and 95.3214°. The peaks can be assigned to the planes of Miller Indexed (100), (002), (101), (102), (112), (201), and (211). The XRD pattern of Fe3O4@ZnO nanoparticles exhibits the peaks assigned to ZnO nanoparticles (101) and Fe3O4 (311), indicating the presence of ZnO nanoparticles on the surface of magnetite nanoparticles. The absence of other peaks for impurities in the patterns indicates the phase purity of the materials.

The XRD pattern of the Fe3O4/ZnO–C nanocomposite shows the maximum diffraction peak at an angle of 2θ = 35.527°. The formed phases of the Fe3O4/ZnO–C nanocomposite are Fe3O4, ZnO, and C. The ICDD data with a reference code of 01-088-0315 showed that the Fe3O4 phase has a cubic crystal structure with lattice parameters of a = b = c = 8.3750 Å. While ICDD data with a reference code of 00-001-1136 showed that the ZnO phase has a hexagonal wurtzite structure with lattice parameters of a = b = 3.2420 Å and c = 5.1760 Å. The ICDD data with a reference code of 01-073-5918 showed that phase C has a rhombohedral structure with lattice parameters of a = b = 2.5158 Å and c = 10.2090 Å. This finding means that the presence of carbon does not change the crystal structure of Fe3O4@ZnO, and carbon does not damage the Fe3O4@ZnO core-shell layer because the carbon is only coupled to the ZnO surface. The FTIR results can also confirm this conclusion, which shows the presence of carbon in the Fe3O4@ZnO–C nanocomposite. The peaks of the Fe3O4 and ZnO still exist in Fe3O4@ZnO-CQD nanocomposites, revealing that the phase of the Fe3O4 and ZnO particles does not change after copolymerization with CQD. The disappearance of some Fe3O4 and ZnO peaks on the Fe3O4@ZnO-CQD nanocomposite, the decrease in diffraction intensity, and the broadening of the diffraction peaks indicate a reduced crystal size of Fe3O4 and ZnO.

The functional groups obtained from FTIR with a range of wavenumbers of 400–4000 cm-1 can be seen in Fig. 2 . The three nanocomposite samples show the formation of ZnO through Zn–O bonds at a wavenumber of 435 cm-1 and Fe3O4 through Fe–O bonds at a wavenumber range of 474.49 nm-1 to 574.79 nm-1. In wavenumbers 545.86 cm-1 and 484.14 cm-1, there are vibrations of tetrahedral Fe–O and octahedral Fe–O bonds, and at 424.35 cm-1, wavenumbers show Zn–O bonds [18]. Fe3O4@ZnO-CQD has similar absorption peaks to Fe3O4@ZnO–C but with a stronger absorption. The O–H bond was found at 3362.68 cm-1 and 3309.86 cm-1 for Fe3O4@ZnO–C and Fe3O4@ZnO-CQD, respectively. The 1633.71 cm-1 band can be assigned to the C=C stretching vibration of the numerous sp2 species enriching the aromatic structure of the CQDs in the Fe3O4@ZnO-CQD. The band at 1591.27 cm-1 is assigned to the C=C stretching of polycyclic aromatic structures in the Fe3O4@ZnO-CQD. Wavenumbers of 2112.05 cm-1 and 2090.84 cm-1 indicate the formation of CC bonds in the sample. The C–O vibrations of the oxygen-containing groups in the Fe3O4@ZnO–C and Fe3O4@ZnO-CQDs were observed at 1337.63 cm-1 and 1360.17 cm-1, respectively. These bonds result from the CQDs interaction with the ZnO [19].

TEM characterization was carried out to determine the core-shell structure of the sample, which was represented by Fe3O4@ZnO, as shown in Fig. 3 . The morphology of the TEM shows the spherical shape of Fe3O4 nanoparticles with a diameter around 15 nm. Fe3O4 nanoparticles are identified with black particles, while the ZnO around Fe3O4 identified is gray. These findings were also obtained in the reported study [11,20]. Fig. 4 a shows the TEM of the Fe3O4@ZnO–C nanocomposite, while Fig. 4b shows the Fe3O4@ZnO-CQD nanocomposite. As seen in Fig. 4a, C occupy the empty space between Fe3O4@ZnO and the others, the same thing can be seen in Fig. 4b, where CQD occupy the space between Fe3O4@ZnO and the others.

The room temperature luminescence properties were studied using PL spectroscopy (Fig. 5 ). The PL spectrum of Fe3O4@ZnO exhibits a UV emission peak at ∼380 nm and a broad visible emission peak from 550 to 800 nm. Free exciton (FE) emissions dominate the UV emission band. The UV emission peak is generally attributed to the band-to-band transition. Several photoluminescence emission centers in the visible region depend on ZnO's vacancies and surface defects in the Fe3O4@ZnO sample. The emissions in the region from 550 nm to 800 nm are commonly referred to as “deep-level emissions” (DLE). The deep-levels of emission are caused by allowed levels inside the band gap of the ZnO that produce transitions with energy in the visible range of the spectrum [21,22]. The appearance of a strong and broad emission peak in the visible region suggests the presence of a higher concentration of defects on ZnO.

The photoluminescence of Fe3O4 @ ZnO–C significantly increases in intensity, especially in the visible region. Fe3O4@ZnO has a similar broad emission peak in the visible light range of about 550 nm–800 nm. The similarity in emission peak indicates that the two samples have the same photoluminescence mechanism. The highest emission peak is around 650 nm, and the orange emission peak is caused by oxygen interstitials (Oi) and zinc interstitials (Zni). Interstitial levels are located 0.22 eV below the conduction band, so the transition between the Zni and Oi levels is about 2.06 eV and produces orange emission [23–25]. The presence of carbon atoms in this sample causes high photoluminescence in Fe3O4@ZnO–C. In addition, the increase in luminescence intensity due to C-doping can improve surface states and enhance the number of defects and oxygen vacancies in ZnO surfaces.

On the other hand, the emission peak of Fe3O4@ZnO-CQD nanocomposite is very different from those of Fe3O4@ZnO and Fe3O4@ZnO–C. There is an increase in the intensity of photoluminescence and a widening of the emission peaks from the near band edge (NBE) UV region to the visible light region, namely at a wavelength of 450 nm–550 nm. Meanwhile, the visible light emission peak decreased and shifted to the near-infrared wavelength region at 800 nm–950 nm. It can be concluded that the photoluminescence mechanism at Fe3O4@ZnO-CQD nanocomposite is different from that of Fe3O4@ZnO and Fe3O4@ZnO–C. It is possibly related to the synthesis process of the samples. Thus, the photoluminescence enhancement of the Fe3O4@ZnO-CQD in the FE UV emission to the green emission region may be caused by ZnO coupling with the CQD's surface plasmon resonance [26,27]. Another possibility is that the green emission with a broad peak centered at 480 nm is inherently associated with various types of structural defects of ZnO, such as oxygen vacancies, zinc vacancies, and zinc interstitials [28]. The photoluminescence intensity in the region of 800–950 nm (near-IR) is more dominated by CQD because of photogenerated electrons and holes trapped at various surface sites and their associated radiative recombination. Fe3O4@ZnO-CQD show high photoluminescence and stability, as they still show fluorescence after ten months, which makes them a suitable candidate for fluorescent probes in fluorescence detection [1].

In conclusion, modification of the Fe3O4@ZnO nanocomposite can improve its photoluminescence properties. The XRD results show the formation of Fe3O4 and ZnO phases without any impurity phases found in the nanocomposite and the formation of a core-shell structure, which the TEM results can confirm. The addition or coupling of the Fe3O4@ZnO nanocomposite with carbon, either with graphite or with carbon quantum dots (CQD), significantly increases photoluminescence. The addition of carbon increases the intensity of the photoluminescence in the yellow-orange wavelength region, with the peak intensity at 670 nm. The coupling of the Fe3O4@ZnO nanocomposite with CQD also increases the intensity of the photoluminescence in the green wavelength region, with a peak wavelength of around 480 nm. The intensity peak appears in the near-infrared wavelength region. The next challenge is to increase the photoluminescence of this nanocomposite material in the near-IR wavelength region. These results prove that this nanocomposite material has the potential to be developed as a bioimaging material.

ce:section.ce:para: Astuti: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Resources, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Syukri Arief: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Mulda Muldarisnur: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Zulhadjri: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Sri R.A. Usna: Validation, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Universitas Andalas according to research contract No: T/23/UN.16.17/PT.01.03/IS-RPT/2022.
